title: Roti
subtitle: Unleavened wholemeal flatbread for the stovetop
excerpt: Six rotis made from wholemeal flour and water, cooked on a dry tawa or cast-iron pan in under 30 minutes total. The kind of bread made every evening across millions of kitchens. No yeast, no oven, no fat in the dough.
sourceNotes: Roti, also known as chapati in British-Indian kitchens, is an unleavened wholemeal flatbread with roots across the Indian subcontinent and East Africa. It is the daily bread of hundreds of millions of people. The technique is consistent across traditions: wholemeal dough, rested briefly, rolled thin, and cooked on a dry griddle. This recipe follows the established domestic tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Stack cooked rotis with baking paper between each one and freeze flat. Reheat in a dry pan over medium heat for 30 seconds a side.
recipe.batchNotes: The recipe doubles or triples cleanly. Rest the full batch of dough together, then roll and cook in sequence.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be made up to 2 hours ahead and left covered at room temperature. Cooked rotis kept in a clean cloth stay soft for up to 2 hours.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: dry tawa or cast-iron skillet, medium-high heat; the pan is ready when a drop of water evaporates in under 2 seconds

glossaryTerms.definition: Chapati dough should be soft and smooth, slightly tacky but not sticky. Press it with a finger: it should spring back slowly. If it springs back fast, the gluten needs more rest. If it sticks to your hands, add a little more flour.

content.text: Roti is bread at its plainest: wholemeal flour, water, a little salt, a hot dry pan. The skill is in reading the
and the pan together. The dough should feel soft and yielding after resting. The pan must be properly hot before the first roti goes on.

Combine 300 g wholemeal flour and 3 g salt in a bowl. Add 170 ml lukewarm water, a little at a time, mixing after each addition. The dough should come together into a soft, smooth ball. Knead for 5 minutes, pressing and folding, until it feels elastic. Cover with a damp cloth and rest at room temperature for 15 minutes. This rest relaxes the gluten so the dough rolls thin without springing back.

Divide the rested dough into 6 equal pieces, about 78 g each. Keep the unrolled pieces covered with the damp cloth to prevent drying. On a lightly floured surface, roll each piece into a circle about 20 to 22 cm across and 2 mm thick. Rotate the dough a quarter turn between each pass of the rolling pin for an even circle. Dust with a little flour if it sticks.

Heat a dry cast-iron skillet or tawa over medium-high heat until very hot. Place one roti in the pan. Cook for 45 to 60 seconds until small bubbles form on the surface and the underside has dry, pale patches. Flip. Cook for another 30 to 45 seconds. The roti should puff slightly and have a few golden-brown spots on each side. Transfer to a clean cloth and fold over to keep soft. Cook the remaining rotis in the same way. The pan stays hot enough between rotis; do not add oil or butter.

items.problem: Roti comes out stiff and cracker-like
items.solution: The pan was too cool or the roti overcooked. A roti cooks in under 2 minutes on a properly hot pan. Keeping cooked rotis in a cloth traps steam and keeps them soft.
The dough needs more rest. Cover and leave for another 10 minutes; the gluten will relax further. Do not roll thinner than 2 mm on the first attempt.
